fast.summary: SmartANNS is a near-data processing system that runs approximate nearest neighbor search directly on SmartSSDs, enabling billion-scale vector search without requiring TB-scale RAM. It uses hierarchical indexing, dynamic task scheduling, and learned shard pruning to achieve near-linear scaling across multiple SmartSSDs.
fast.key_idea: By co-locating computation with storage on SmartSSDs and using a host+SSD cooperative architecture with hierarchical indices, SmartANNS dramatically reduces data movement and achieves scalable ANNS at a fraction of the memory cost.
fast.why_it_matters: Billion-scale vector search typically demands expensive high-memory servers; SmartANNS offers a cost-effective alternative using commodity SmartSSDs, making large-scale semantic search and retrieval accessible without specialized memory-heavy infrastructure.
possible_use_cases: Large-scale semantic search and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems where memory cost is a bottleneck
Recommendation engines requiring real-time similarity search over billion-item catalogs
Database systems needing efficient high-dimensional vector indexing without dedicated in-memory hardware

deep.method_breakdown: SmartANNS uses a hierarchical indexing approach where host CPUs maintain a coarse-grained index to prune the search space before dispatching tasks to SmartSSDs, which run fine-grained ANNS locally near the data. A dynamic task scheduler optimizes data layout across multiple SmartSSDs to enable load balancing and data reuse, reducing redundant I/O. A learning-based shard pruning algorithm further eliminates unnecessary SmartSSD computations by predicting which shards are unlikely to contain nearest neighbors for a given query.
deep.key_innovation: The cooperative 'host CPUs + SmartSSDs' hierarchical architecture is the core novelty — prior SmartSSD-based ANNS (e.g., CSDANNS) pushed all computation to SSDs without a host-side coarse filter, causing wasted computation. The learning-based shard pruning is also novel: instead of static partitioning heuristics, a trained model decides at query time which shards to skip, adapting to query distribution.
deep.technical_insight: The most important design choice is the hierarchical index split: host CPUs handle a lightweight coarse index (e.g., clustering centroids) to identify candidate shards, then only those shards are activated on SmartSSDs. This works because the coarse index fits in DRAM and eliminates the majority of shards before any SSD computation, dramatically reducing both I/O bandwidth pressure and FPGA/ARM compute cycles on the SSD — the bottleneck in NDP architectures.
deep.limitations: The learning-based shard pruner is trained on a query distribution and may degrade on out-of-distribution queries, reducing recall without warning. The architecture assumes Samsung SmartSSD hardware, limiting portability. Near-linear scaling is demonstrated but the communication overhead between host and multiple SSDs may become a bottleneck at very large SSD counts. The hierarchical index adds preprocessing complexity and the coarse index must still fit in host DRAM, which can be limiting for extremely high-dimensional datasets.
interview_questions: What is the core architectural difference between SmartANNS and prior SmartSSD-based ANNS solutions like CSDANNS?
How does the learning-based shard pruning algorithm work, and what is its risk?
Why is near-linear scaling with multiple SmartSSDs non-trivial, and how does SmartANNS achieve it?
What role does data layout optimization play in SmartANNS's performance?
How does the cooperative host CPU + SmartSSD architecture reduce the compute burden on SSDs?
interview_answers: SmartANNS introduces a hierarchical split where host CPUs run a coarse-grained index to prune candidate shards before dispatching work to SmartSSDs, whereas CSDANNS sends queries to all SSDs without host-side filtering — this eliminates wasted computation on irrelevant shards.

A learned model is trained to predict, given a query vector, which data shards are unlikely to contain the true nearest neighbors and can be skipped. The risk is distributional shift: if query patterns at inference differ from training data, the model may incorrectly prune relevant shards, silently lowering recall.

Near-linear scaling is hard because naive sharding causes load imbalance (some SSDs get more hits than others) and redundant data access. SmartANNS solves this via dynamic task scheduling that monitors SSD load at runtime and an optimized data layout that co-locates frequently co-accessed vectors to enable data reuse across queries.

Optimized data layout clusters vectors that tend to be co-retrieved (e.g., neighbors in embedding space) onto the same SSD shard, increasing cache/buffer hit rates within a SmartSSD and reducing redundant reads. This directly reduces I/O amplification, which is the dominant cost in storage-class NDP systems.

The host CPU's coarse index (e.g., IVF cluster centroids) rapidly identifies the top-k candidate shards for each query using only a small DRAM-resident structure. Only those shards are activated on the SmartSSDs, so each SSD performs fine-grained search on a small fraction of the dataset — reducing both memory bandwidth and compute cycles per query on the resource-constrained SSD processors.
